elements.sent-2411-001: Deep Learning models tend to learn correlations of patterns on huge datasets.
elements.sent-2411-002: The bigger these systems are, the more complex are the phenomena they can detect, and the more data they need for this.
elements.sent-2411-003: The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming increasingly ubiquitous in our society, and its impact is growing everyday.
elements.sent-2411-004: The promises it holds strongly depend on their fair and universal use, such as access to information or education for all.
elements.sent-2411-005: In a world of inequalities, they can help to reach the most disadvantaged areas.
elements.sent-2411-006: However, such a universal systems must be able to represent society, without benefiting some at the expense of others.
elements.sent-2411-007: We must not reproduce the inequalities observed throughout the world, but educate these IAs to go beyond them.
elements.sent-2411-008: We have seen cases where these systems use gender, race, or even class information in ways that are not appropriate for resolving their tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Instead of real causal reasoning, they rely on spurious correlations, which is what we usually call a bias.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we first attempt to define what is a bias in general terms.
elements.sent-2411-011: It helps us to demystify the concept of bias, to understand why we can find them everywhere and why they are sometimes useful.
elements.sent-2411-012: Second, we focus over the notion of what is generally seen as negative bias, the one we want to avoid in machine learning, before presenting a general zoology containing the most common of these biases.
elements.sent-2411-013: We finally conclude by looking at classical methods to detect them, by means of specially crafted datasets of templates and specific algorithms, and also classical methods to mitigate them.
elements.sent-2411-014: Fairness in Artificial Intelligence (AI) models is a huge concern for society today.
elements.sent-2411-015: Biased algorithms are shaking up society in ways we never imagined, often reinforcing existing inequalities and discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-016: Imagine an algorithm deciding who gets a loan, who gets hired, or even who gets bail.
elements.sent-2411-017: If these algorithms are biased, they can unfairly target certain groups, like minority communities or women, perpetuating injustice and discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-018: In criminal justice, biased algorithms can lead to higher incarceration rates for minorities, while in hiring, they might favor men over equally qualified women.
elements.sent-2411-019: Healthcare is not immune either—biased algorithms can result in poorer treatment recommendations for underrepresented groups, with dire consequences.
elements.sent-2411-020: The ethical implications of these biases extend beyond direct discrimination to issues of accountability and transparency.
elements.sent-2411-021: These algorithms often operate as black boxes, making their decision-making processes opaque and unchallengeable.
elements.sent-2411-022: This lack of transparency erodes trust and raises serious accountability issues.
elements.sent-2411-023: Who’s responsible when an algorithm discriminates? The answer isn’t simple, but it starts with diversifying the data, implementing bias detection techniques, and fostering collaboration among technologists, ethicists, and policymakers.
elements.sent-2411-024: Raising public awareness about these digital biases is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-025: While algorithms promise efficiency and innovation, their ethical deployment must prioritize fairness and justice to ensure they benefit everyone equally without deepening existing societal divides.
elements.sent-2411-026: As AI becomes increasingly pervasive, the quest for high performance drives models to become more complex, often relying on correlational associations.
elements.sent-2411-027: Yet, we also demand that these models exhibit unbiased behavior, respecting diversity and relying on causation rather than correlation.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, let's not forget that biases are everywhere, as almost nothing in the world is pure randomness, structures are everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-029: Mathematics can be defined as the study of structures, but similar structures are sharing a common structural bias, which can complicate the effort to use them without perpetuating unfair correlations.
elements.sent-2411-030: This creates a significant dilemma: how can we leverage the useful priors of the world to make probable decisions without falling into the trap of non-causal biased correlations that can be harmful in some sensitive cases? For example, the crime rate is higher is certain subgroups in the population, a probabilistic pick is not a causal reason to know that an individual will commit a crime even though it might be statistically probable regarding hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-031: It is a common practice called racial profiling, which sanctions based on social and ethnics prejudices, and leads to an unfair treatment and systemic bias in law enforcement practices \citep{Jounin2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The challenge lies in removing such detrimental biases from AI models without sacrificing their ability to make informed, probabilistic decisions based on the world's inherent structures.
elements.sent-2411-033: In other words: how to remove detrimental biases in AI models?.
elements.sent-2411-034: Ensuring fairness in machine learning (ML) is a complex and challenging task.
elements.sent-2411-035: Firstly, ML models are trained on real-world data, which inherently contains biases related to race, gender, religion, social class, or whatever.
elements.sent-2411-036: As a result, these models can not only learn but also amplify these pre-existing biases \citep{Hall2022 , leading to problematic outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Secondly, despite rigorous and comprehensive training and testing, creating systems that behave fairly across all situations and cultures remains a significant challenge \citep{Mehrabi2021,Santy2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: For instance, consider a chatbot trained on Castilian Spanish.
elements.sent-2411-039: It might perform well when interacting with users from Spain, but might struggle to understand and respond appropriately to evolving slang used by teenagers.
elements.sent-2411-040: When used by speakers from Chile or Argentina, the chatbot could have difficulty adapting to the unique idioms and new polysemy of those regions, resulting in miscommunication and frustration (or laughter, but poor performances).
elements.sent-2411-041: Even within its initial context, such as among Castilian speakers, the chatbot might encounter problems with specific dialects or local events, revealing unexpected and unfair outcomes post-deployment.
elements.sent-2411-042: Thirdly, defining fairness is inherently complex, as there is no universally accepted standard, whether for human or machine decisions.
elements.sent-2411-043: Determining appropriate fairness criteria for a system requires balancing user experience, cultural, social, historical, political, legal, and ethical considerations, each with potential trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-044: Even in seemingly straightforward situations, there can be disagreement over what constitutes fairness, as it can rely on the values of the individuals \cite{Sorensen2023,Mirzakhmedova202 , complicating the establishment of AI policies, especially in a global context.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Finally, generative AI is becoming an increasingly important part of our lives, and generates more and more content on the internet, which is the primary source of data for training new models.
elements.sent-2411-046: The inherent bias of contemporary generative models is a poison for future generative models, as it will lower the quality of the future training data \cite{Taori2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Nonetheless, striving for continuous improvement towards "fairer" systems is essential, and even if absolute fairness remains elusive, there are ways to tend to it by removing the biases of existing models.
elements.sent-2411-048: But before talking about debiasing IA, let's start simple: what is a bias? We will show in the following that bias have many senses, starting from its various definitions and uses in the language.
elements.sent-2411-049: First, let’s think about the mathematical bias of a linear model.
elements.sent-2411-050: This is the value that a model will output when the inputs are zero.
elements.sent-2411-051: A model is then biased when it outputs a value different than zero when transforming the vector null.
elements.sent-2411-052: It is the deviation that the model introduces from the zero element.
elements.sent-2411-053: This bias helps the model to fit the data, because every data is biased.
elements.sent-2411-054: Second, let’s think about cognitive biases.
elements.sent-2411-055: These are biases that all humans are sharing.
elements.sent-2411-056: They can be seen as a difference between the reality and our perception of the world.
elements.sent-2411-057: They can be very low-level like a simple optical illusion,\footnote{which acts very much likes cognitive bias \cite{Baer2017 or of higher-level of complexity, constructed on social concepts.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: At lower-level of complexity, you can see an example in Figure \ref{fig:chess a chessboard with a cylinder on it: the dark squares outside the shadow looks darker than the white squares in the shadow, even though in reality they have the same dark grey color.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Another case where we can separate groups of people regarding their bias is the famous gold and white vs black and blue dress.\footnote{If you need a reminder, it's right \href{https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/2/21/The\_dress\_blueblackwhitegold.jpg {here.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: At higher-level of complexity, we can mention among others the Dunning-Kruger effect, the availability bias, or the confirmation bias.
elements.sent-2411-061: The Dunning-Kruger effect, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:dk_effect , is a the tendency for an individual with limited knowledge or competence in a given field to overestimate their own skills in that field.\footnote{Confirmation bias is the tendency for the brain to value new information that supports existing ideas, and availability bias is the tendency for the brain to conclude that a known instance is more representative of the whole than is actually the case.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: One of the greatest popularization book about human cognitive biases is "Thinking fast and slow" from \citet{kahneman2011thinking , who won a Nobel Prize in Economy for his life work on psychology of judgment and decision-making.}
elements.sent-2411-063: These biases can be seen as a deviation between our perception and the reality.
elements.sent-2411-064: Third, we can name the social biases, like social norms which are cultural biases.
elements.sent-2411-065: It can be as simple as the proper way to dress yourself, but also involves behavioral aspects.
elements.sent-2411-066: The expectations from people of different social groups will vary with respect to the group, depending on what it is expecting from one of its members.
elements.sent-2411-067: When two individuals from different cultures do not know the social norms of the other ones, they can experience what is called a culture clash.
elements.sent-2411-068: Culture clash is defined by Jonathan H.
elements.sent-2411-069: Turner\footnote{Professor of sociology at University of California, Riverside as "differences in cultural values and beliefs that place people at odds with one another".}
elements.sent-2411-070: In some culture, saying "no" can be seen as a sign of weakness, when in some others as a sign of strength.
elements.sent-2411-071: Note that social norms depend on social groups, so you can even find differences within one culture, norms can change regarding the social group: you cannot have the same kind of behavior as a man or as a woman in many countries.
elements.sent-2411-072: Biases are not fundamentally bad, they just are a deviation from a (subjective and defined) norm or value.
elements.sent-2411-073: For example, cognitive biases come from the structure of our brain, which is not random.
elements.sent-2411-074: We all share a significant portion of our DNA, which could be seen as unbiased if it was center of zero: a random sequence of nucleotides,\footnote{A, T, G and C like a distribution centered over zero.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Differences in hair, eye color, skin tone, nose or mouth shape can reflect phenotypic variations.
elements.sent-2411-076: Phenotypes include similar patterns in DNA: again, individuals from the same subgroup share the same biases when compared to outside subgroups.
elements.sent-2411-077: We rely on biases every time we take a decision, it is terribly useful in order to select the most probable choices.
elements.sent-2411-078: In their ACL paper, \citet{Meister2022 show that an intuitive bias for a language model\footnote{A model supposed to predict next word knowing the precedent ones, such as what you have when written a text on your smartphone is the actual use frequency of the different words.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: It reflects the way of speaking, which is a bias: you know that an Argentinean and Chilean will not use the same words.
elements.sent-2411-080: For this, they simply initialized the bias term of the language model’s final linear layer with the log-unigram distribution of the words.
elements.sent-2411-081: This helps using prior knowledge to minimize the loss in a very naive way, and links the two notions of bias we introduced before!.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Zoology of common biases in science
elements.sent-2411-082: It exists a complete zoology of biases, they come from a gap between the perception and the truth.
elements.sent-2411-083: They can in fine impact the validity and reliability of findings which lead to misinterpretation of data.
elements.sent-2411-084: More biases are described \href{https://www.scribbr.com/category/research-bias/ {in this article .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: This bias occurs when the frequency of events, properties, and outcomes recorded in a dataset does not accurately reflect their real-world prevalence.
elements.sent-2411-086: It often arises because people tend to document unusual or memorable circumstances, assuming that ordinary events are not worth noting: the phenomenon exists but people do not report it.
elements.sent-2411-087: At another level, it can result from the tendency to publish only successful experiments or positive results, which creates a skewed perception of a model’s effectiveness and mislead about its true capabilities and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-088: It is the tendency to prefer results produced by automated systems over those produced by non-automated systems, regardless of the respective error rates.
elements.sent-2411-089: That is how you arrive to a lousy chatbot not understanding a user's special request, which should be handled by a human.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.4\textwidth]{selection_bias.png} \caption{An example of coverage bias from the xkcd comics \href{https://xkcd.com/2618/}{\#2618}} \label{fig:selection_bias}
elements.sent-2411-090: It occurs if a dataset's examples are chosen in a way that is not reflective of their real-world distribution.\footnote{It is different than reporting bias This bias can take various forms.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The coverage bias arises when some groups are inadequately represented in the training data, like surveying in a Computer Science classroom in order to poll about how much people know about programming (see Figure \ref{fig:selection_bias ).}
elements.sent-2411-092: The participation bias occurs when only the people interested will answer a study and the sampling bias is due to a non-randomization of the answers (or of the data during the training!).
elements.sent-2411-093: This bias is a bit similar to the selection bias but with a subtlety.
elements.sent-2411-094: It happens when the data collected only represent a subgroup of the population, even though it represents the reality.
elements.sent-2411-095: The fact that mostly males are CEOs does not mean that gender is a feature to success as a CEO.
elements.sent-2411-096: It involves overgeneralizing characteristics, based on limited observations of individuals, to the entire group to which they belong.
elements.sent-2411-097: It can be an in-group bias, which tend to favor the individuals from the same group as the experimenter, or out-group homogeneity bias which will tend perceiving members of an out-group as more similar to each other than they actually are, like a Castilian-speaking data scientist making one category for Castilian-Spanish and another for the rest of the variations.
elements.sent-2411-098: This bias occurs when assumptions are made based on one's own mental models and personal experiences that do not necessarily apply more generally.
elements.sent-2411-099: It is called a confirmation bias when one processes data in ways that affirm preexisting beliefs and hypotheses, such as discarding out-of-distribution without cause, or experimenter's bias when one conditions the experiment to reach the expected conclusion.\footnote{An example of experimenter's bias is depicted \href{https://xkcd.com/882/ {here .}}}

elements.sent-2411-139: For tabular data, a missing value can be the place of a bias.
elements.sent-2411-140: If some values are missing from a speciific target group, then it may indicate it is under-represented.
elements.sent-2411-141: For example, if the income data for a particular demographic is often missing, the model might misinterpret the economic status of that group.
elements.sent-2411-142: Unexpected feature values could signal data entry errors or other inaccuracies leading to a bias.
elements.sent-2411-143: A negative age for some people might bias the model against certain age groups.
elements.sent-2411-144: Moreover, identifying unexpected values helps in spotting outliers which may disproportionately represent a minority group.
elements.sent-2411-145: If the model is trained on these outliers without correction, it might generalize poorly on them.
elements.sent-2411-146: If certain groups or characteristics may be under- or over-represented relative to their real-world prevalence, it can introduce a bias into the model.
elements.sent-2411-147: And even if they do represent the real-world prevalence, it might not be the best idea to train on them and repeat real-world inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-148: Looking at the data distribution with respect to the different target groups is a good option.
elements.sent-2411-149: It is an easy way to check if there is an important skewness in the represented data between the different target groups.
elements.sent-2411-150: Similarly, the number of samples per target-group is also important to not favor the over-represented group.

elements.sent-2411-192: It is important to first detect their impacts, which is possible using some of the methods we presented in this paper, and second minimize them in order to tend to fairer IA models.
elements.sent-2411-193: This is possible through optimizing specific objectives with loss functions, adversarial learning, or using data-augmentation, etc...
elements.sent-2411-194: This step is essential if we are to fulfill the promises of AI for a fairer world.. \bibliographystyle{natbib.}
